assertions.description: Use-As-Dictionary can carry an explicit ttl=0 parameter.
assertions.test: (() => { const h = new Headers(); h.set('Use-As-Dictionary', 'match="/page-*", ttl=0'); return /(^|[, ]+)ttl=0(,|$)/.test(h.get('Use-As-Dictionary')); })()

assertions.description: ttl=0 makes a dictionary ineligible for subsequent requests even when no time has elapsed.
assertions.test: (() => { const isDictionaryFresh = (ttl, age) => ttl > 0 && age < ttl; return isDictionaryFresh(0, 0) === false && isDictionaryFresh(60, 0) === true; })()

assertions.description: An explicit ttl can expire dictionary usefulness before the HTTP cache max-age expires.
assertions.test: (() => { const age = 120; const ttl = 60; const cacheMaxAge = 3600; return age >= ttl && age < cacheMaxAge; })()

assertions.description: An explicit ttl can keep dictionary usefulness separate from the resource's HTTP cache lifetime.
assertions.test: (() => { const age = 7200; const ttl = 86400; const cacheMaxAge = 3600; return age < ttl && age >= cacheMaxAge; })()

assertions.description: When ttl is omitted, the fallback model follows the HTTP cache lifetime.
assertions.test: (() => { const fallbackFresh = (cacheMaxAge, age) => age < cacheMaxAge; return fallbackFresh(3600, 3599) === true && fallbackFresh(3600, 3600) === false; })()

assertions.description: The Available-Dictionary request header is only modeled when the selected dictionary lifetime is fresh.
Headers can preserve the dcb and dcz content-coding tokens used when a fresh dictionary is available.
assertions.test: (() => { const h = new Headers({ 'Accept-Encoding': 'gzip, br, dcb, dcz' }); const value = h.get('Accept-Encoding'); return value.includes('dcb') && value.includes('dcz'); })()
